coredata.dc:title: Z-scheme CoS/Bi4O5I2 heterostructure for visible light catalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride: Degradation mechanism, toxicity evaluation, and potential applications

Peng, Xiaojun
Zhang, Rui
Chen, Yadong
Dai, Xianwei
Wang, Wenling
coredata.dc:description: The Z-type heterojunction CoS/Bi4O5I2 material was prepared by the solvothermal method. The crystal structure and surface elemental valence state of the material were investigated by XRD, XPS, and other characterization methods. The microstructure of the material was investigated by SEM, BET, and other methods. In the experiment of degradation of 20 mg/L tetracycline hydrochloride using the prepared photocatalytic materials, the optimal photocatalytic performance of CoS/Bi4O5I2 was explored with the introduction of 1.5 % CoS, and the degradation rate could reach 94.7 %. It was also found that 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 had stable photocatalytic performance and good photocatalytic performance for all tetracycline antibiotics. Then the possible degradation pathways in the degradation process of TCH were explored using GC-MS, and the acute toxicity of the intermediates was evaluated by ECOSAR software. The UV–vis-DRS results showed that the overall photoelectrical properties of the composites were enhanced after introducing CoS with broad-spectrum absorption properties, and the PL and EIS results also proved the collation. The possible degradation mechanism of the CoS/Bi4O5I2 system was also speculated by combining the free radical trapping experiments with the M-S curve results. The increasing severity of antibiotic pollution necessitates the development of highly efficient photocatalytic materials to enhance environmental remediation capabilities. Traditional photocatalysts suffer from high recombination rates of photogenerated carriers and low degradation efficiency. To enhance the photocatalytic activity and cycling stability of Bi4O5I2, we synthesized a CoS/Bi4O5I2 heterojunction material via a solvothermal method. Using 20 mg/L TCH as a model pollutant, we investigated the photocatalytic efficiency of the prepared material. Through comprehensive experiments and structural analyses using XRD, XPS, and SEM, we explored the photocatalytic degradation performance and mechanism of tetracycline, confirming that CoS/Bi4O5I2 is an ideal material for constructing a Z-type heterojunction, effectively reducing the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Compared to pure Bi4O5I2, the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite exhibits superior performance in improving charge separation efficiency and enhancing visible light absorption. This study successfully constructs a Z-type heterojunction with efficient electron-hole separation, significantly enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The composite material holds great potential for antibiotic pollution treatment, while the elucidation of its photocatalytic degradation mechanism provides theoretical guidance for designing novel photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Z-type heterojunction CoS/Bi4O5I2 material was prepared by the solvothermal method. The crystal structure and surface elemental valence state of the material were investigated by XRD, XPS, and other characterization methods. The microstructure of the material was investigated by SEM, BET, and other methods. In the experiment of degradation of 20 mg/L tetracycline hydrochloride using the prepared photocatalytic materials, the optimal photocatalytic performance of CoS/Bi4O5I2 was explored with the introduction of 1.5 % CoS, and the degradation rate could reach 94.7 %. It was also found that 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 had stable photocatalytic performance and good photocatalytic performance for all tetracycline antibiotics. Then the possible degradation pathways in the degradation process of TCH were explored using GC-MS, and the acute toxicity of the intermediates was evaluated by ECOSAR software. The UV–vis-DRS results showed that the overall photoelectrical properties of the composites were enhanced after introducing CoS with broad-spectrum absorption properties, and the PL and EIS results also proved the collation. The possible degradation mechanism of the CoS/Bi4O5I2 system was also speculated by combining the free radical trapping experiments with the M-S curve results.

The increasing severity of antibiotic pollution necessitates the development of highly efficient photocatalytic materials to enhance environmental remediation capabilities. Traditional photocatalysts suffer from high recombination rates of photogenerated carriers and low degradation efficiency. To enhance the photocatalytic activity and cycling stability of Bi4O5I2, we synthesized a CoS/Bi4O5I2 heterojunction material via a solvothermal method. Using 20 mg/L TCH as a model pollutant, we investigated the photocatalytic efficiency of the prepared material. Through comprehensive experiments and structural analyses using XRD, XPS, and SEM, we explored the photocatalytic degradation performance and mechanism of tetracycline, confirming that CoS/Bi4O5I2 is an ideal material for constructing a Z-type heterojunction, effectively reducing the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Compared to pure Bi4O5I2, the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite exhibits superior performance in improving charge separation efficiency and enhancing visible light absorption. This study successfully constructs a Z-type heterojunction with efficient electron-hole separation, significantly enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The composite material holds great potential for antibiotic pollution treatment, while the elucidation of its photocatalytic degradation mechanism provides theoretical guidance for designing novel photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing severity of antibiotic pollution, particularly the impact of tetracycline hydrochloride on aquatic ecosystems, there is an urgent need to develop efficient and environmentally friendly catalytic materials to degrade these hazardous substances. Bismuth oxide halides (BixOyXz, X = I, Br, Cl, F) are considered a potential class of photocatalytic materials due to their special layered crystal structure and stable optical properties (Qi et al., 2022). To date, a series of bismuth oxide halide materials have been developed to improve light trapping efficiency and photocatalytic performance (Xiong et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2024; Elamin et al., 2023; Zhen et al., 2024). Due to their strong oxidizing ability, high photochemical stability, and unique layered structure, bismuth oxide halide materials show great potential for photocatalytic decomposition of environmentally hazardous substances (Lan et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2021a; Chu et al., 2023; Qiu et al., 2024; Nkudede et al., 2024). Among them, BiOI has been widely studied due to its recyclability, non-biotoxicity, simple synthesis, and low cost (Yang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024; Ma et al., 2023; Ahmed et al., 2024; Chu et al., 2022; Niyati et al., 2020). However, due to its weak redox properties, there are some limitations in practical application. In recent years, it has been found that the band gap of bismuth oxide can be adjusted by changing the ratio of Bi and I to improve its photocatalytic performance. Bi4O5I2 obtained by thermal decomposition has higher photocatalytic activity than BiOI (Sun et al., 2018; Cai et al., 2024; Yan et al., 2021b; Feng et al., 2024). However, it remains problematic that the compounding of photogenerated carriers reduces the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction. Previous studies have shown that charge separation can be enhanced by combining different materials to form heterojunction structures (Gao et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2024; He et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023). Therefore, combining other semiconductors with Bi4O5I2 to form heterogeneous photocatalysts is a relatively simple and effective measure to broaden the visible light absorption and further improve its photocatalytic performance. Hu et al. reported the photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) using oxygen-rich and highly visible light-responsive Bi4O5I2/Ag3PO4 (Liu and Jin, 2020). Shen et al. reported the preparation of Bi4O5I2/Bi5O7I with enhanced photocatalytic activity against the antibiotic tetracycline (Shen et al., 2021).

In recent years, transition metal sulphides such as cobalt sulfide, nickel sulphide, molybdenum sulphide, and copper sulphide have been commonly used in photocatalytic degradation (Shen et al., 2022; Gong et al., 2023; Chien et al., 2023; Alakshar et al., 2024; Mantilla et al., 2025; Zou et al., 2024). In particular, cobalt sulfide (CoS), as a typical sulfur-containing compound, has been studied with increasing interest due to its excellent light absorption performance, low cost, low toxicity, and narrow bandgap (Luo et al., 2023; Lai et al., 2023), and Park et al. showed that doping a small amount of CoS quantum dots on TiO2 can help to increase the visible light absorption capacity of TiO2 and the electron-hole Park et al. Liu et al. combined CoS with ZnWO4 as a nano-photocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen production, and the formation of CoS/ZnWO4 heterojunctions increased hydrogen yield (Park et al., 2021). Therefore, combining CoS with Bi4O5I2 photocatalysts to form heterojunctions can improve the photocatalytic performance of the materials, and the heterojunction photocatalytic system can provide a simpler and more direct pathway for photogenerated carrier transfer and separation (Liu et al., 2020).

In order to enhance the photocatalytic activity of Bi4O5I2, we prepared CoS/Bi4O5I2 heterojunction materials by compositing CoS with Bi4O5I2 using the solvothermal method. The photocatalytic efficiency of the prepared materials was investigated using 20 mg/L TCH as a simulated pollutant. The detailed experimental analysis and characterization of the materials confirm that CoS/Bi4O5I2 is an ideal material for the formation of Z-type heterojunctions, which can effectively reduce the complexation of photogenerated electrons and holes. Compared with Bi4O5I2 alone, CoS/Bi4O5I2 shows superior performance in improving the photogenerated charge separation efficiency and enhancing the absorption of visible light. In addition, the structural morphology and energy band structure of the composites, as well as the degradation pathway of TCH, toxicity assessment of the intermediates, and degradation mechanism are thoroughly analyzed in this paper.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate(Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), Ethylene glycol(CH2OH)2), Cobalt chloride hexahydrate(CoCl·6H2O), Thioacetamide(CH3CSNH2), Ethanol absolute(C2H6O), Tetracycline Hydrochloride(C22H25ClN2O8) all the above reagents are analytically pure and come from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. The deionized water was self-prepared in our laboratory.

The electronic analytical balance (E6315) was manufactured by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The constant temperature magnetic stirrer (KW-1000dc) was produced by Shanghai Jinfu Instrument Co., Ltd. The electric blast drying oven (DHG-9070A) was supplied by Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd. The high-speed centrifuge (TG16G) was manufactured by Jintan Gaoke Instrument Factory. The box-type muffle furnace (SX-4-10) was produced by Beijing Zhongxing Weiye Century Instrument Co., Ltd. The UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600) was manufactured by Shimadzu Corporation, Japan. The scanning electron microscope (EVO MAI5) was produced by Carl Zeiss Optics Co., Ltd., Germany. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250XI) was manufactured by Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA. The X-ray diffractometer (Xpert Pro, MPD) was supplied by PANalytical, Netherlands. The fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS55) was manufactured by PerkinElmer, USA. The transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100) was produced by JEOL Ltd., Japan. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (Lambda 850) was manufactured by PerkinElmer, USA. The electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) was supplied by Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.

Firstly, 1.94 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 30 mL of ethylene glycol as solution A. Then, 0.66 g of KI was dissolved in 20 mL of purified water as solution B. Solution B was slowly added dropwise into A and stirred at room temperature for 1.5 h. After that, the product was centrifuged and washed three times with purified water and ethanol respectively. The cleaned product was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h. After drying, the product was ground and transferred to a quartz crucible and calcined in a muffle furnace at 410 °C for 2 h at a rate of 3 °C/min. The final product was obtained as Bi4O5I2. The product of Bi4O5I2 was then added to a quartz crucible at room temperature.

After 0.2 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O, 0.4 mmol of thioacetamide, and a fixed amount of Bi4O5I2 were dissolved in 40 mL of ethanol, the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30 min, then it was transferred to 100 mL of the inner liner, and the reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 12 h. At the end of the reaction, the products were washed three times with deionized water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. CoS/Bi4O5I2 was obtained by the following reaction. After the reaction, the products were washed three times with deionized water and ethanol and finally dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. To obtain CoS/Bi4O5I2, the composites were obtained in different proportions (0.5 %, 1 %, 1.5 %, and 2 %) according to the addition of different amounts of Bi4O5I2 (3.64 g, 1.82 g, 1.21 g, and 0.91 g). In the above preparation method, Bi4O5I2 was not added, and the product obtained was a CoS monomer. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 .

In order to investigate the crystal structures of the samples, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to investigate the crystal structures of CoS, Bi4O5I2, and 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of these photocatalysts. The peaks at 30.6°, 35.3°, 47.1°, and 54.5° correspond to the (100), (101), (102), and (110) crystal planes of CoS, respectively, and the positions of these peaks are well-matched with the standard card of CoS, PDF#65–3418. In contrast, the XRD peaks of Bi4O5I2 are located at 28.5°, 31.4°, 36.3°, 45.0°, 48.7°, and 54.2°, which correspond to the (-4-11), (402), (−404), (422), (006), and (811) crystal planes. The XRD peaks observed in the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites coincide with the diffraction peaks of Bi4O5I2 (Liu et al., 2023), which suggests that Bi4O5I2 maintains the integrity of its crystalline structure when bound to CoS. XRD analysis also confirmed that the synthesized material is of high purity and no other impurities were found. In the XRD patterns of the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite, the CoS specific diffraction peaks are not as obvious as those of Bi4O5I2. This may be due to the lower loading of CoS on the surface of Bi4O5I2, the better dispersion, and the low crystallinity of CoS.

The elemental compositions and chemical states of CoS, Bi4O5I2, and 1.5°% CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites were investigated by XPS analysis, and the elements of Bi, O, I, Co, and S were detected in Fig. 3 (a) for 1.5°% CoS/Bi4O5I2, which indicates that the materials were successfully prepared. Fig. 3(b) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f. 1.5°% CoS/Bi4O5I2 shows two different peak signals at 164.15 and 158.8 eV, which correspond to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, respectively, and indicate that the Bi element exists in the form of Bi3+. The positions of the two characteristic peaks of Bi 4f of the composite are shifted compared to Bi4O5I2. In Fig. 3(c), for pure Bi4O5I2, the signals of the peaks at 531.35 eV and 529.75 eV are attributed to the Bi-O and O-H bonds, respectively. Similar to the Bi element, the signals of the two peaks in the 1.5°% CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite are shifted to 531.30 eV and 529.95 eV. The I 3d spectra show two peaks at 630.45 eV and 618.95 eV (Fig. 3(d)), which represent the I 3d3/2 and I 3d5/2. The signals of the Co 2p spectra in Fig. 3(e) are 781.45 eV and 797.1 eV, respectively. 797.1 eV are Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively, and 786.75 eV and 806.75 eV correspond to the two satellite peaks, which indicates the presence of Co in the form of Co2+ (Xiao et al., 2014). In Fig. 3(f), the peaks at 162.9 eV and 161.25 eV are attributed to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively, which suggests that the S element exists in the form of S2−, whereas the peak at 168.8 eV is attributed to the sulphate species, and this change may be due to the oxidation or adsorption of thioacetamide intermediates during hydrothermal treatment (Zhu et al., 2014). Similar to the Bi, O elements, the characteristic peaks of the other elements in the composites are shifted compared to the peak signals of the monomers, which suggests that there is a strong interaction between CoS and Bi4O5I2, and the chemical environments of all the elements are altered, supporting the formation of Z-type heterojunctions.

The specific surface area of a photocatalyst is one of the factors affecting its adsorption capacity and photocatalytic efficiency. The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of Bi4O5I2 and 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites are shown in Fig. 4 (a), and both materials exhibit type IV isotherms with significant H3 type hysteresis loops, which is indicative of the presence of mesoporous structures within them (Fu et al., 2018), and Fig. 4(b) further shows that the pore size distributions of these materials are between 2 and 50 nm which further confirms the presence of mesoporous structures. Based on the BET equation, the specific surface area of the composite 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 (12.3746 m2/g) was calculated to be increased compared to the specific surface area of the Bi4O5I2 monomer (10.3290 m2/g), and thus the composite material was able to provide more surface-active sites during the photocatalytic process, which resulted in the contaminants being in full contact with the material, thus improving its photocatalytic effect.

The microstructures of the materials were investigated by SEM, which proved that CoS was successfully loaded on the surface of Bi4O5I2, and CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites were successfully formed. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the prepared CoS monomer is a hexagonal sheet with a side length of about 1 μm, and the prepared Bi4O5I2 is a spherical material with a diameter of about 2 μm, as shown in Fig. 5(b). As can be seen from Fig. 5(c)(d), the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites were uniformly deposited on the Bi4O5I2 spheres, forming a three-dimensional CoS/Bi4O5I2 photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the uniform distribution of Co, S, Bi, O, and I in the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites not only indicates that there is no other elemental impurity in the material but also shows that CoS is uniformly distributed on Bi4O5I2. At the same time, it also shows that the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites are successfully prepared.

To provide further evidence for the successful synthesis of the heterojunction, a TEM analysis was carried out, the results of which are shown in Fig. 5(f). As can be seen in Fig. 5(a) and (b), CoS has a lamellar structure, while Bi4O5I2 exhibits a flowery spherical structure. The high-resolution TEM clearly shows that CoS presents a rod-like structure and is attached to Bi4O5I2.In the TEM image, two kinds of lattice stripes can be observed, and the crystal plane spacing of the two kinds of lattice strip. The crystal plane spacing of these stripes is measured at 0.304 nm and 0.277 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (−411) crystal plane of Bi4O5I2 and the (101) crystal plane of CoS.

In order to investigate the ability of the prepared materials to degrade organic pollutants, the photocatalytic efficiency of various samples was evaluated under visible light irradiation using TCH as a simulated pollutant. Prior to the visible light irradiation, the adsorption process was carried out for 30 min in the absence of light to reach the equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. Subsequently, after 90 min of visible light irradiation, the removal effects of different materials on TCH are shown in Fig. 6 (a), where the removal efficiencies of TCH were 21 % by CoS and 64.2 % by Bi4O5I2. Compared with the two nanomaterials, the removal efficiencies of TCH by the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite photocatalysts were greatly improved, and the removal efficiencies of TCH by the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite photocatalysts in the different ratios were greatly improved. Among the different ratios of CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite photocatalysts, the removal efficiency of 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 was the highest, and the UV absorption spectra of the degradation of TCH by 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2, which had a removal efficiency of 94.7 %, are shown in Fig. 6(b). The composite of CoS with Bi4O5I2 showed a significant increase in the degradation efficiency of TCH, suggesting that a transport channel for photogenerated carriers might be formed between the two monomers. Additionally, the degradation rate of TCH was investigated using the proposed one-stage kinetic calculation. The 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 photocatalyst showed the best photocatalytic activity, with a reaction rate constant k value of 0.03388 min–1, which was 21.4 times higher than that of the CoS monomer (0.00157 min−1), and 3.7 times higher than that of Bi4O5I2 (0.00922 min−1). The above experiments showed that the photocatalytic degradation ability of the material could be greatly improved by the combination of CoS and Bi4O5I2 monomers. In the subsequent experiments, 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 was selected as the primary composite material.

Experiments were conducted with different photocatalyst dosages and pollutant concentrations, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 (a)(b). When the photocatalyst dosage was 50 mg, the pollutant degradation rate reached 94.7 %. However, when the dosage increased to 60 mg, the degradation rate declined due to the shielding effect caused by excessive photocatalyst loading. As the pollutant concentration increased, its degradation rate decreased. The TOC removal efficiency was evaluated to further assess the photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 7(c), TCH was mineralized into CO2 and H2O. The TOC removal rate of 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 is significantly higher than that of pure Bi4O5I2, indicating excellent synergy between CoS and Bi4O5I2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability performance evaluation of photocatalytic materials
ce:section.ce:para: In reality, the reusable and stable properties of photocatalysts are important parameters to measure their comprehensive performance. In order to investigate whether the prepared CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite photocatalysts are stable in nature, the 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites exploring the optimal amount of CoS introduction were collected and centrifuged dried after the degradation of TCH was completed and the degradation experiments were repeated for salt TCH. Keeping the same experimental conditions, five repetitive experiments were carried out, and the experimental data are shown in Fig. 8 (a), which shows that the degradation effect of 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 after five consecutive experiments each time remained relatively stable, and the photocatalytic efficiency of the 5th degradation experiment only decreased by 3.2 % compared with the first experiment. By comparing the XRD of the 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites before and after cycling, it was found that Fig. 8(b), the peaks of the XRD of the composites before and after cycling were still one-to-one, and the crystalline phases did not change significantly, which further proved the stability of the 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the toxicity of intermediates and intermediates within the photocatalytic process
ce:section.ce:para: In order to gain a deeper understanding of the degradation process of 1.5°%CoS/Bi4O5I2 photodegradation of TCH, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) was used to identify the possible intermediates formed and a possible photodegradation pathway was hypothesized (Fig. 9 (a)), whereby the photocatalyst generates free radicals, which tend to attack the functional groups on the TCH molecule that have a high electron density, leading to the degradation of TCH. The mass-to-charge ratios of the intermediates (P1-P7) formed during the degradation of TCH are 342, 208, 144, 84, 193, 135, and 83, respectively, and their structures are shown in Fig. 9(a). First, the four rings of the pollutant TCH are gradually opened and transformed into other cyclic compounds by decarboxylation and substitution reactions, and then the last ring opens up and cracks into linear organic molecules, which are further converted into water, carbon dioxide, and other organic compounds. These linear molecules are further converted into water, carbon dioxide and inorganic salts.

From Fig. 9(b), it can be seen that the toxicity of P1 and P3 to fish, Daphnia, and green algae is slightly higher than that of TCH, the toxicity of P2 to green algae is stronger than that of TCH, but the toxicity of P2 to fish and Daphnia is weaker, the toxicity of P6 to Daphnia is stronger than that of TCH, while the toxicity of P6 to fish and green algae is lower than that of TCH, and the toxicity of P4, P5, and P7 to the three kinds of organisms is much lower than that of TCH. During the photodegradation process, the concentration of TCH will gradually decrease, and the intermediate products will be lower, so the average acute toxicity of the photocatalyst system will be reduced.

The UV–vis DRS technique can be used to analyze the light absorption properties of the materials and from this, the band gap (Eg) of CoS and Bi4O5I2 is calculated. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), CoS exhibits a wide range of light absorption properties. The absorption band of Bi4O5I2 is located at 503 nm, whereas the absorption edge of 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 after the introduction of 1.5 % CoS is shifted to 560 nm, which implies that the light absorption ability of the composite material 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 in the visible region is significantly strengthened when compared to pure Bi4O5I2 has been significantly enhanced, which allows more efficient use of visible light. In addition, based on these UV–Vis DRS data, the band gap energy (Eg) of Bi4O5I2, CoS can be deduced from the following Kubelka-Munk equation:
(1) (αhν)n = A(hν- Eg)
Where α is the absorption coefficient, hv is the photon energy, and A is the constant associated with the material. For the indirect semiconductor Bi4O5I2, the exponent n is 1/2; for CoS, which is a direct semiconductor CoS, n takes the value of 2. As shown in Fig. 10(b)(c), the final values of the forbidden bandwidths for CoS and Bi4O5I2 are calculated to be 1.74 eV and 2.25 eV, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of photoelectric properties of materials
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence spectroscopy is a measure of the efficiency of photogenerated charge separation in photocatalytic systems. Usually, if the photoluminescence spectrum signal is not strong, it means that the generated electrons and holes are separated more efficiently (Zhang and Liu, 2020). Fig. 11 (a) shows the PL spectra of the catalysts of the prepared materials under 320 nm excitation. It is observed that CoS and Bi4O5I2 exhibit strong PL spectral signals, which implies a faster rate of photogenerated electron-hole pair complexation in these two catalysts. The PL spectral intensity of 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 is significantly lower than that of the other samples, which indicates that the 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 binary composites can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers and thus improve the photocatalytic performance.

This result is further verified by the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the materials, as shown in Fig. 11(b). The arc radius of 1.5°%CoS/Bi4O5I2 is significantly smaller than that of Bi4O5I2 and CoS, and the smaller the arc diameter, the lower the resistance to electron transfer in the EIS (Di et al., 2015), which suggests that 1.5°%CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites encountered less resistance in the electron migration and its photogenerated electron-hole pair dispersion is higher, which is consistent with the results of PL spectroscopy. This indicates that the 1.5°%CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites encounter less resistance to electron migration and their photogenerated electron-hole pairs are dispersed more effectively, which is consistent with the results of PL spectroscopy.

The transient photocurrent responses under intermittent visible light irradiation were measured for CoS, Bi4O5I2, and 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2. As shown in Figs. 11(c), 1.5 %CoS/Bi4O5I2 exhibited the highest transient photocurrent intensity compared to pure CoS and pure Bi4O5I2, indicating that the synergistic effect between CoS and Bi4O5I2 enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of 1.5°%CoS/Bi4O5I2

ce:section.ce:para: In order to gain a deeper understanding of the photocatalytic mechanism of the composites, in this study, the Mott-Schottky test was carried out by an electrochemical workstation to understand the conduction band potential (ECB) and the valence band potential (EVB) of CoS and Bi4O5I2. The slope of the M-S curve reflects the type of semiconductor: positive slopes are for n-type semiconductors and negative slopes are for p-type semiconductors. The M-S curves for CoS and Bi4O5I2 shown in Fig. 12 both have positive slopes, which means that both are n-type semiconductors. As shown in Fig. 12(a)(b), the flat band potentials (Efb) of CoS and Bi4O5I2 are −0.31 V and −0.98 V (vs. SCE). The ECB of CoS and Bi4O5I2 can be calculated as −0.27 eV and −0.94 eV, respectively, based on ECB = Eg + ECB and the forbidden bandwidths Eg of CoS and Bi4O5I2 calculated in Fig. 10(b) and (c) The EVB of CoS and Bi4O5I2 are 1.47 eV and 1.31 eV, respectively.

In order to thoroughly investigate the photocatalytic mechanism of CoS/Bi4O5I2 photocatalyst in the degradation of TCH and to identify the active species generated in the reaction, photocatalytic active species capture experiments were carried out. Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ) were selected as capture agents and used as trapping h+, ·OH, and·O2 −. The capture results of the three trapping agents are presented in Fig. 13 . Among them, the addition of IPA had almost no inhibitory effect on the degradation of TCH, which indicated that there was almost no role of ·OH in the photocatalytic degradation system. In contrast, the degradation efficiency of TCH was drastically reduced to 63.4 % and 40.9 % with the addition of EDTA-2Na and BQ, which indicated that the active species acting in the photocatalytic treatment of TCH included h+ and ·O2 −, with O2 − being the main active species.

EPR analysis was conducted to further confirm that ·O2− and h+ are the primary active species in the photocatalytic reaction system of the Z-type heterojunction CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 14 (a), TEMPO was used as the trapping agent for h+, and the continuous generation of h+ was observed both in the dark and under light illumination. Using DMPO as the trapping agent for ·O2−, no distinct ·O2− characteristic signals were detected in the dark or after 5 min of illumination, as shown in Fig.(b). However, with increasing illumination time, a significant ·O2− characteristic signal appeared at 15 min. Therefore, it can be concluded that a large amount of h+ and ·O2− are generated during the photocatalytic degradation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism of the material CoS/Bi4O5I2
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above experimental and test results, the possible degradation mechanism for the degradation of TCH by 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 composites is demonstrated in Fig. 15 . Specifically, in a conventional type II heterojunction, if 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 is taken as an example, e− will be transferred from the conduction band of Bi4O5I2 to the conduction band of CoS, while h+ will be migrated from the valence band of CoS to the valence band of Bi4O5I2. However, the conduction potential of CoS is −0.29 eV, which is higher than the reduction potential of O2/·O2- (−0.33 eV vs NHE). This implies that the e-accumulated on the CoS conduction band is not enough to efficiently reduce oxygen to ·O2 −. Whereas, based on the analysis of free radical trapping experiments, it was concluded that ·O2 − is the dominant active species in the photocatalytic treatment process. Therefore, it can be inferred that 1.5 % CoS/Bi4O5I2 is not a typical type II heterojunction structure, but rather a Z-type heterojunction with photogenerated electron transfer. Under visible light irradiation, photogenerated electrons in the CoS semiconductor are transferred to the valence band of Bi4O5I2 and recombine with holes in Bi4O5I2's valence band, preserving the holes in the valence band of CoS and the electrons in the conduction band of Bi4O5I2. The ECB of Bi4O5I2 reaches −0.94 eV, which is higher than that of the reduction of the O2/·O2 − potential (−0.33 eV vs NHE), suggesting that the e− on the Bi4O5I2 conduction band can convert O2 to ·O2 −, a process that not only reduces the binding of electrons to holes but also generates ·O2 − with strong oxidizing, a species that can contribute to the decomposition of pollutants. Meanwhile, the holes in the CoS valence band contribute to the degradation of organic pollutants into smaller organic molecules through redox reactions. Eventually, these organic molecules are completely converted into water, carbon dioxide, and inorganic salts.

CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite photocatalytic materials were prepared using the solvothermal method. The successful preparation of the material was confirmed by XRD, XPS, SEM, EDS, TEM, and ESR, which demonstrated the close binding of CoS and Bi4O5I2. BET analysis revealed that the CoS/Bi4O5I2 composite exhibited a larger specific surface area, providing more photocatalytic active sites. In the degradation experiment of 20 mg/L TCH, it was found that the composite had the highest degradation efficiency of 94.7 % and good cyclic stability when the introduction of CoS was 1.5 %. Meanwhile, the degradation products were analyzed by GC-MS, and the possible degradation pathways of TCH were proposed. The toxicity assessment of the degradation intermediates led to the conclusion that the acute toxicity of the photocatalytic system was reduced. Based on the experimental results of the degradation of the three tetracycline antibiotics, TCH, OTC, and CTC, by 1.5°%CoS/Bi4O5I2, the composite photocatalysts proved to have good general applicability. In addition, the composites exhibited superior photogenerated electron-hole pair separation and transfer efficiency as evidenced by PL and EIS mapping, and the UV–vis DRS spectroscopy results indicated that the visible absorption boundary of the composites has been significantly improved compared with that of Bi4O5I2 monomer. Finally, the results of free radical trapping experiments indicated that ·O2 − and h+ were the main active species, and a suitable Z-type CoS/Bi4O5I2 photocatalytic degradation mechanism was deduced by combining the energy band structures calculated from the UV–vis DRS spectra as well as the valence band values and conduction band values of CoS with Bi4O5I2 derived from the M-S curves.

In terms of practical significance, this study has developed a novel and highly efficient photocatalyst, providing a feasible technological solution to the global environmental challenge of antibiotic pollution. Due to its low synthesis cost, simple preparation process, strong degradation capability, and broad applicability, this composite material holds significant potential for industrial wastewater treatment and water purification. The research findings offer new insights into the design of heterojunction photocatalytic materials while bridging the gap between scientific research and practical applications in antibiotic pollution control, contributing to substantial socio-economic benefits and ecological environmental protection.
